quality.rationale: 基于霍金面积定理的不等式推导与极值分析，逻辑链完整且具天体物理启发性

origin.question: [文件 1: 1_s17_17.14.md]\\n\\nProblem 17.14. According to Hawking's theorem (that in a collision of two black holes the total surface area must not decrease), what is the minimum mass $\\mathbf{M}_2$ of a Schwarzschild black hole that results from the collision of two Kerr black holes of equal mass $\\mathbf{M}_1$ and opposite angular\\nmomentum parameter, a? Suppose $|a| \\approx M_1$ ; what fraction of the original mass can be radiated away? Are there any other uncharged black-hole collisions that can get this much energy out?
origin.answer: Minimum mass: $\\mathbf{M}_2 = \\mathbf{M}_1$; Fraction radiated: $50\\%$; Other collisions: No, this is the maximum efficiency for uncharged black-hole collisions.
origin.solution: Solution 17.14. The two initial black holes have the same area, so the total initial surface area $(Q = 0$ in Problem 17.13) is\\n$$\\n$\\mathbf {A}_{\\mathbf {i}} = 1 6 \\pi \\mathbf {M}_{\\mathbf {1}} \\left[ \\mathbf {M}_{\\mathbf {1}} + \\left(\\mathbf {M}_{\\mathbf {1}}^{2} - a^{2}\\right)^{\\frac {1}{2}} \\right].\\n$$\\nThe final black hole is a Schwarzschild black hole, so\\n$$\\n$\\mathbf {A_{f}} = 4 \\pi (2 \\mathbf {M}_{2})^{2} = 1 6 \\pi \\mathbf {M}_{2}^{2}.\\n$$\\nThe inequality $\mathbf{A}_{\\mathbf{f}} \\geq \\mathbf{A}_{\\mathbf{i}}$ becomes\\n$$\\n$\\mathbf {M}_{2}^{2} \\geq \\mathbf {M}_{1}^{2} + \\mathbf {M}_{1}(\\mathbf {M}_{1}^{2} - \\mathbf {a}^{2})^{\\frac {1}{2}}.\\n$$\\nIf $a = M_1$ , this means $M_2 \\geq M_1$ . The total initial mass was $2M_1$ , so the final mass must be at least half of this. According to Hawking's theorem then, up to $50\\%$ of the original mass can be radiated away!\\nThis is the most efficient of the collisions without charge. Suppose two black holes of masses $\mathbf{M}_1$ and $\mathbf{M}_2$ , and angular momentum parameters $\mathbf{a}_1$ and $\mathbf{a}_2$ coalesce to form a black hole of mass $\mathbf{M}_3$ , and parameter $\mathbf{a}_3$ ; then $\mathbf{A}_{\\mathrm{f}} \\geq \\mathbf{A}_{\\mathrm{i}}$ implies that\\n$$\\n$\\mathsf {M}_{1} \\left[ \\mathsf {M}_{1} + (\\mathsf {M}_{1}^{2} - \\mathsf {a}_{1}^{2})^{\\frac {1}{2}} \\right] + \\mathsf {M}_{2} \\left[ \\mathsf {M}_{2} + (\\mathsf {M}_{2}^{2} - \\mathsf {a}_{2}^{2})^{\\frac {1}{2}} \\right] \\leq \\mathsf {M}_{3} \\left[ \\mathsf {M}_{3} + (\\mathsf {M}_{3}^{2} - \\mathsf {a}_{3}^{2})^{\\frac {1}{2}} \\right].\\n$$\\nOne gets the most energy out when the equality obtains. Moreover, for given $\mathbb{M}_1$ , $\mathbb{M}_2$ , $\mathbf{a}_1$ , $\mathbf{a}_2$ the smallest $\mathbb{M}_3$ (hence the largest radiation of energy) will occur for $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ . Similarly, for given $\mathbb{M}_3$ (with $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ ), the largest $\mathbb{M}_1 + \mathbb{M}_2$ (hence the largest radiation of energy) will occur for $|\mathbf{a}_1| = |\mathbf{a}_2| = \mathbb{M}_1 = \mathbb{M}_2$ . Since $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ , conservation of angular momentum implies that $\mathbf{a}_1 = -\mathbf{a}_2$ . This is just the situation we worked in detail above. The conclusion, therefore, is that when two Kerr holes collide and coalesce, no more than $50\\%$ of their original mass can be radiated away!
